nativeRange.habitat: Reef slopes, reef flats, and sandy lagoon bottoms in shallow tropical reef zones; typically rests free on sandy substrate rather than attached to rock

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Benefits from target-feeding meaty foods — micro-plankton, brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, or small meaty pieces — roughly once weekly. Tentacles are generally extended during daylight and can actively capture zooplankton.

avoidWith: other corals placed within tentacle reach (8-15 cm)
Fungia sp. (short tentacle plate corals) — may compete for same sand-bed territory

imageCandidates.notes: Diego Delso, 3361x3361. Sharp in-situ reef shot; whole free-living disc in frame with the diagnostic long, knob-tipped mauve/pink tentacles clearly shown. Clean, no watermark.
Dan Schofield, 2048x1536. Striking vivid green colour morph, side profile showing plate body and white-tipped tentacles. Small corner watermark; adds a distinct colour variant.
Joao D'Andretta, 1445x2048. Sharp top-down view of the full tan disc with radiating pale-tipped tentacles; different angle and colour from the others.

summary: The Long Tentacle Plate Coral (Heliofungia actiniformis) is a free-living LPS coral whose tentacles — up to roughly 15 cm when extended — closely resemble a sea anemone. Best suited for intermediate reefers; it requires a sandy bottom, moderate lighting, and gentle flow.
careNotes:
Heliofungia actiniformis is distinctive among Fungiidae for its unusually long tentacles — generally reported at 8–15 cm when extended — which give the coral a strong visual resemblance to a large sea anemone. It is sometimes confused with anemones by beginning hobbyists. Unlike most stony corals, it is free-living (not attached to substrate) and should be placed directly on a sandy bottom rather than on live rock. It can move slowly across the sand bed over time by inflating and deflating its body.

Key care points:
• Placement: Sandy substrate in the lower third of the aquarium. The coral needs clear space in all directions — sweeper tentacles can extend well beyond its visible disc and will sting nearby corals. Allow at least 15–20 cm of clear space around the specimen.
• Lighting: Moderate; generally advised to keep under 75–150 PAR. High-intensity direct lighting can bleach the colony. Acclimate gradually if moving from a lower-light environment.
• Flow: Low to gentle indirect flow is preferred. Strong direct flow will cause the coral to retract and may injure tissue. Gentle random tumbling flow (not laminar) is suitable.
• Feeding: Has been reported to respond well to weekly target-feeding of meaty foods (brine shrimp, mysis, chopped seafood) delivered directly to the tentacles when extended. Zooxanthellae provide a substantial portion of metabolic needs under adequate light.
• Aggression: The long sweeper tentacles carry a potent sting and tend to remain extended during daylight hours, making this coral a significant threat to corals placed within reach. It is widely considered one of the more aggressive LPS corals for this reason, despite its anemone-like appearance suggesting passivity.
• Water chemistry: Standard reef parameters are generally sufficient. Supplemental calcium, strontium, and trace elements support skeletal growth. Sudden changes in alkalinity or salinity tend to cause rapid tissue recession.
• Distinct from sibling genus: Heliofungia (one species: H. actiniformis) has significantly longer, knob-tipped tentacles compared to Fungia sp. (short tentacle plate corals), a helpful distinction when identifying specimens in the trade.
breedingNotes: On natural reefs, the Long Tentacle Plate Coral reproduces sexually through broadcast spawning. Larval rearing has been studied in laboratory settings, but captive propagation is not practiced in the home aquarium hobby. Fragging is not straightforward for free-living members of family Fungiidae — unlike branching corals, there is no convenient branch to cut. The species is occasionally propagated through natural fission or in controlled aquaculture facilities, but specimens sold in the hobby are almost entirely wild-collected.

additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family Fungiidae, Order Scleractinia), native range (eastern Indian Ocean, central Indo-Pacific, Australia, Japan, South China Sea, West Pacific islands), depth range (1–25 m), adult size (disc up to 20 cm diameter, 7 cm height), and confirming zooxanthellate + zooplankton predator feeding strategy.
